coredata.dc:title: A comparative study of the singlet oxygen generation capability of a zinc phthalocyanine linked to graphene quantum dots through π-π stacking and covalent conjugation when embedded in asymmetric polymer membranes
coredata.dc:creator: Mafukidze, Donovan M.
Nyokong, Tebello
coredata.dc:description: Following the establishment of graphene quantum dots as potential phthalocyanine supports in photoactive membrane preparation for phthalocyanines lacking linkage functional groups, the practical efficiency of π-π stacking versus covalent linkage was investigated. Synthesized materials were characterized using FT-IR, Raman, powder X-ray diffraction, and UV–Vis spectroscopies and also by transmission electron and scanning electron microscopies. Phthalocyanine loadings onto graphene quantum dots of 0.40 mg/mg and 0.14 mg/mg (Pc mass/conjugate mass) for the π-π stacked and covalent linked conjugates respectively were observed. Covalent linkage to graphene quantum dots proved to be functionally superior to π-π linkage, where singlet oxygen quantum yield value of the phthalocyanine in the membrane for the covalent linked conjugate was approximately twice that of the π-π stacked membrane.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Phthalocyanines
Singlet oxygen quantum yield
Covalent conjugation
Polymer membranes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Embedding (π)Pc-GQDs-(COOH)PS, (c)Pc-GQDs-(COOH)PS and Pc-(NH2)PS into unfunctionalized PS polymer to form polymer membranes
Synthesis and characterization of GQDs nanoconjugates
Synthesis and characterization of PS conjugates
Synthesis and characterization of functionalized PS membranes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India
Department of Science and Technology, Republic of South Africa

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology ( DST ) Innovation, National Research Foundation ( NRF ) South Africa and Sasol Inzalo Foundation through DST / NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology ( UID 62620 ) as well as Rhodes University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM micrograms and size distribution histograms of (a) GQDs (b) (π)Pc-GQDs and (c) (c)Pc-GQDs.
FT-IR spectra of Pc, GQDs, (π)Pc-GQDs, (c)Pc-GQDs, (COOH)PS, (NH2)PS, Pc-(NH2)PS, (π)Pc-GQDs-(COOH)PS and (c)Pc-GQDs-(COOH)PS.
FT-Raman spectra of Pc, GQDs, (π)Pc-GQDs and (c)Pc-GQDs.
XRD diffractograms of Pc, GQDs, (π)Pc-GQDs and (c)Pc-GQDs.
Ground state UV–Vis absorption spectra of GQDs, Pc, (π)Pc-GQDs and (c)Pc-GQDs, and emission spectrum of GQDs in DMSO.
SEM images of (π)Pc-GQDs-membrane edge, skin, top and bottom.
Solid state UV–Vis absorption spectra of Pc-membrane, (c)Pc-GQDs-membrane and (π)Pc-GQDs-membrane tops.
UV–Vis spectral changes observed during the photolysis of DPBF (a) by (π)Pc-GQDs in DMSO and (b) by (π)Pc-GQDs-membrane in ethanol.

(a) SEM images of (π)Pc-GQDs-membrane before and after repeated cycles of light irradiation of membrane suspended in ethanol and (b) solid state UV–Vis spectra of upper membrane surface before and after repeated cycles of light irradiation with membrane suspended in ethanol.
Pc-GQDs-(COOH)PS conjugate synthesis. rt = room temperature.
Summary of photophysicochemical parameters of synthesized material in DMSO unless otherwise stated.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Values in brackets are TEM sizes (nm).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: λabs = absorption maxima, λem = emission maxima.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Values in round brackets are for exciting where GQDs absorb (otherwise the values are where Pc absorbs). Numbers in square brackets are references.

head.ce:title: A comparative study of the singlet oxygen generation capability of a zinc phthalocyanine linked to graphene quantum dots through π-π stacking and covalent conjugation when embedded in asymmetric polymer membranes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Rhodes University, PO Box 94, Grahamstown, 6140, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Following the establishment of graphene quantum dots as potential phthalocyanine supports in photoactive membrane preparation for phthalocyanines lacking linkage functional groups, the practical efficiency of π-π stacking versus covalent linkage was investigated. Synthesized materials were characterized using FT-IR, Raman, powder X-ray diffraction, and UV–Vis spectroscopies and also by transmission electron and scanning electron microscopies. Phthalocyanine loadings onto graphene quantum dots of 0.40 mg/mg and 0.14 mg/mg (Pc mass/conjugate mass) for the π-π stacked and covalent linked conjugates respectively were observed. Covalent linkage to graphene quantum dots proved to be functionally superior to π-π linkage, where singlet oxygen quantum yield value of the phthalocyanine in the membrane for the covalent linked conjugate was approximately twice that of the π-π stacked membrane.

ce:section.ce:para: The use of metallophthalocyanines (MPc) as photosensitizers for photocatalytic environmental remediation and in particular for water treatment has continued to grow [1–7]. In photocatalysis an excited photosensitizer in its triplet state transfers energy to molecular oxygen generating singlet oxygen and other reactive oxygen species (ROS) which degrade the pollutants. Singlet oxygen is a particularly good candidate for these applications. Thus it is important to quantify and improve singlet oxygen yields. Phthalocyanines have been shown to be highly effective photosensitizers capable of producing singlet oxygen with high quantum yields [8]. ZnPc derivatives are known for their high singlet oxygen generation ability [8], hence a ZnPc derivative is employed in this work. For practical applications, MPcs have been linked to or embedded in supports to aid catalyst retrieval [5–7,9–11]. Electrospun fibers are often used as supports [5–7], however electrospun fibers tend to lose their characteristics following use, hence not allowing for re-use in many cases [6]. In earlier work we introduced the use of asymmetric polymer membranes as Pc supports in photocatalysis with good re-usability of the membrane [11]. Hence membranes containing a ZnPc derivative are employed in this work.

Pcs have been employed as photocatalysts when adsorbed onto graphene quantum dots (GQDs), followed by embedding into a polymer membrane [12], with improved photocatalytic behaviour. In addition, porphyrins (similar in structure to phthalocyanines) showed improved photocatalytic behaviour in the presence of GQDs [13]. MPcs easily adsorb onto GQDs since the π electron rich Pc macrocycle readily forms π-π conjugates with other π electron rich material such as GQDs [14,15]. In addition to forming π-π conjugates between GQDs and the ZnPc derivative (represented as (π)Pc-GQDs), we also covalently link the ZnPc derivative to GQDs (represented as (c)Pc-GQDs) before casting into membranes in order to compare the effect of π-π and covalent linking. An asymmetric ZnPc derivative (a monocarboxy phenoxy ZnPc) is employed to allow for a more defined linking. In addition, asymmetry is known to introduce distortion on the phthalocyanine macrocyle which affects the electronic states of the conjugated macrocycle ring, hence the photophysical properties of the Pc [16].

In our previous work [11], we observed leaching of the Pc from the membrane when the Pc was simply mixed with the polymer solution (without prior conjugation) before membrane casting. Hence, (π)Pc-GQDs) and (c)Pc-GQDs) were covalently linked to (COOH)PS (PS = polystyrene), before the formation of the membrane. We evaluate the singlet oxygen generating ability of the ZnPc within (π)Pc-GQDs) and (c)Pc-GQDs) when embedded in membranes.

1,3-Diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF), polystyrene (PS), hydrazine, N,N′-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC) and ZnPc were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Dimethylformamide (DMF), tetrahydrofuran (THF), diethyl ether, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), methanol and ethanol were purchased from Merck. The syntheses of monocarboxyphenoxy substituted ZnPc (Pc) [17], polyethylenimine functionalized GQDs [18] and COOH functionalized polystyrene ((COOH)PS) [11] have been reported.

Ultraviolet–visible spectra (UV–Vis) for solutions were recorded on a Shimadzu UV–Vis 2550 spectrophotometer while the spectra of solid samples were obtained from a Perkin Elmer Lambda 950 UV–Vis NIR spectrophotometer. Infra-red spectra were collected on a Bruker Alpha model FT-IR Spectrometer with platinum-ATR.

Excitation and emission spectra were recorded on a Varian Eclipse Spectrofluorimeter. Fluorescence lifetimes were measured using correlated single photon counting setup (TCSPC) (PicoQuant FluoTime 300). The excitation source was a diode laser (LDH-P-670 (for Pc) driven by PDL 800-B, 670 nm, 20 MHz repetition rate, 44 ps pulse width, Picoquant GmbH). LDH-P-375 diode laser was used for exciting at a wavelength where QDs absorb. The luminescence decay curve was measured at the maximum of the emission peak and the lifetime was obtained by deconvolution of the decay curve using the FluoFit Software program (PicoQuant GmbH, Germany).

A laser flash photolysis system consisting of a LP980 spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments) with a PMT-LP detector and an ICCD camera (Andor DH320T-25F03) was used to determine triplet quantum yields. The signal from a PMT detector was recorded on a Tektronix TDS3012C digital storage oscilloscope. The excitation pulses were produced using a tunable laser system consisting of an Nd:YAG laser (355 nm, 135 mJ/4–6 ns) pumping an optical parametric oscillator (OPO, 30 mJ/3–5 ns) with a wavelength range of 420–2300 nm (NT-342B, Ekspla). The triplet lifetimes were obtained by exponential fitting of the kinetic curves using OriginPro 8 software.

The scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were obtained using a JOEL JSM 840 scanning electron microscope. Transmission electron microscope (TEM), ZEISS LIBRA® model 120 operated at 90 kV was used for the assessment of morphologies of the NPs and their conjugates. Raman spectra were obtained using a Bruker Vertex 70-Ram II (equipped with a 1064 nm Nd:YAG laser and liquid nitrogen cooled germanium detector). A Bruker D8 Discover equipped with a Lynx Eye detector, using Cu-Kα radiation (l = 1.5405 Å, nickel filter) was used to obtain X-ray powder diffraction patterns in the scan range of 2θ = 100 to 1000, at a rate of 0.560 min−1 with a filter time-constant of 2.5 s per step and a slit width of 6.0 mm.

Photo-irradiation for the determination of singlet oxygen quantum yields for conjugates in DMSO solutions was done using a General electric Quartz line lamp (300W). A 600 nm glass cut off filter (Schott) and a water filter were used to filter off ultraviolet and infrared radiations respectively. An interference filter (Intor, 670 nm with a band width of 40 nm) was placed in the light path before the sample. The light intensity was measured with a POWER MAX 5100 (Molelectron Detector Inc.) power meter and found to be 1.0 × 1019 photons cm−2 s−1. Modulight ML7710-680/690-RHO laser system using the 680 nm wavelength probe was used for the determination of singlet oxygen quantum yield for the functionalized membranes suspended in ethanol with DPBF as quencher. A laser system (single wavelength light) was used to photo-irradiate the membranes to enable the calculation of exact amount of energy used since the absolute method was employed in the absence of a reference standard for the determination of singlet oxygen of the Pc in the membrane.

Monocarboxyphenoxy substituted ZnPc (Pcc) was conjugated to GQDs via π-π stacking using a procedure previously reported [14,15]. Briefly: GQDs (24.0 mg in 1 ml water) and Pc (32.0 mg, 0.045 mmol) were dispersed in 2.0 mL dry DMF and sonicated for 16 h. The mixture was then stirred at room temperature for 48 h followed by successive washing with ethanol, deionized water, and then with 1:1 (v:v), THF: ethanol mixture by centrifugation. The product was finally washed with diethyl ether and air dried.

The (π)Pc-GQDs-(COOH)PS conjugate was synthesized by conjugation of (π)Pc-GQDs to (COOH)PS via an amide bond. (COOH)PS (100 mg) and DCC (50 mg, 0.242 mM) were dispersed in dry DMF (2 mL) and stirred for 48 h at room temperature. A solution of 15.0 mg (π)Pc-GQDs in 3.0 mL of dry DMF was added to the reaction mixture and stirring continued for 72 h. The reaction mixture was diluted by addition of 150 mL of methanol and the product ((π)Pc-GQDs-(COOH)PS) collected by centrifugation. The crude product was then washed, also by centrifugation, using ethyl acetate and then a 2:3 (v:v) DMSO:THF solvent mixture. The product was then washed once with diethyl ether and air dried. The conjugate is represented as (π)Pc-GQDs-(COOH)PS.

In the synthesis of (c)Pc-GQDs conjugate (c represents covalent), Pc (24.2 mg, 0.034 mmol) and DCC (14.0 mg, 0.068 mmol) were dispersed in 2.0 ml dry DMF, and the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 48 h. After 48 h, 18 mg of GQDs in 1.5 ml of 1:2 (v:v) water: DMF were added and the reaction allowed to proceed for 72 h. The product was then collected and successively washed with deionized water, ethyl acetate and then with 1:1 (v:v) THF:ethanol mixture, by centrifugation. The product was then air dried.

(c)Pc-GQDs-(COOH)PS conjugate was synthesized from (c)Pc-GQDs and (COOH)PS using the same procedure used for synthesis of (π)Pc-GQDs-(COOH)PS conjugate above. The same purification protocol for (π)Pc-GQDs-(COOH)PS was followed. The conjugate is represented as (c)Pc-GQDs-(COOH)PS.

(NH2)PS was synthesized by modifying (COOH)PS using hydrazine. In the synthesis, (COOH)PS (100 mg) was activated using DCC (50 mg, 0.242 mmol) for 48 h at room temperature in 2 ml of dry DMF. After 48 h, hydrazine (0.5 ml, 15.9 mmol) was added to the reaction mixture and allowed to react for 72 h. After 72 h the reaction mixture was precipitated and washed with acetone several times by centrifugation and air dried.

The Pc-(NH2)PS conjugate was synthesized through the formation of an amide bond between Pc and (NH2)PS as follows: Pc (20 mg, 0.028 mmol) and DCC (15.0 mg, 0.073 mmol) were dispersed in 2.0 ml dry DMF, and the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 48 h. After 48 h, 50 mg of (NH2)PS was added and the reaction allowed to proceed for 72 h. The product was then collected and successively washed with acetone, ethyl acetate and then with 1:1 (v:v), THF: ethanol mixture, by centrifugation. The product was then air dried. The conjugate is represented as Pc-(NH2)PS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Embedding (π)Pc-GQDs-(COOH)PS, (c)Pc-GQDs-(COOH)PS and Pc-(NH2)PS into unfunctionalized PS polymer to form polymer membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The membranes ((π)Pc-GQDs-membrane, (c)Pc-GQDs-membrane and Pc-membrane for (π)Pc-GQDs-(COOH)PS, (c)Pc-GQDs-(COOH)PS and Pc-(NH2)PS, respectively) were prepared as we reported before [11,12]. Briefly (π)Pc-GQDs-(COOH)PS, (c)Pc-GQDs-(COOH)PS and Pc-(NH2)PS (22.8 mg each) and 480.5 mg of unfunctionalized PS were dispersed in dry DMF (2.12 mL) and homogenized by stirring for 24 h. The polymer dispersions (0.60 mL) were then cast on 76 × 26 mm glass slides and immediately added to a methanol precipitation bath to give the (π)Pc-GQDs-membrane, (c)Pc-GQDs-membrane and Pc-membrane at approximately 4.7% catalyst loading.

The fluorescence (
Φ
F
) and triplet (
Φ
T
) quantum yields of the nano-conjugates were determined in DMSO using comparative methods described in literature [19–21]. Fluorescence quantum yields depend on the optical densities and the refractive indices of the sample and the standard. Unsubstituted ZnPc in DMSO was used as a standard with
Φ
F
= 0.20 [20] and
Φ
T
= 0.65 [19] (when exciting where Pc absorbs). When exciting where GQDs absorb quinine sulphate was used as a standard in 0.1 M H2SO4 with
Φ
F
= 0.54 [22]. For triplet state studies, the solutions were de-aerated with argon for 15 min before measurements. The triplet lifetimes were then determined by exponential fitting of the kinetic curves using OriginPro 8 software.

Fluorescence quantum yields of GQDs when conjugated to Pc (
Φ
F ( G Q D s )
C o n j u g a t e
)
were determined using Eq. (1): [23]
(1)
Φ
F
( G Q D s )
C o n j u g a t e
=
Φ
F
( G Q D s )
F
Conjugate
F
GQDs
where
Φ
F
( G Q D s )
(the fluorescence quantum yield of the GQDs alone) and was used as the standard, FGQDs is the integrated fluorescence intensity of the GQDs alone and FConjugate is the integrated fluorescence intensity of the GQDs in the conjugates at the excitation wavelength of the GQDs.

Singlet oxygen quantum yield (Φ Δ ) values for the nanoconjugates were determined in DMSO under ambient conditions with DPBF as a singlet oxygen quencher and calculated using equations described before [19,24]. Unsubstituted ZnPc in DMSO was used as a standard (ΦΔ(Std) = 0.67 in DMSO) [24]. The UV–Vis absorbance spectra of DPBF was then spectroscopically monitored at 417 nm at a predetermined time course. ΦΔ studies for (π)Pc-GQDs-(COOH)PS and (c)Pc-GQDs-(COOH)PS were not done due to lack of solubility. For the functionalized membranes, Φ Δ was determined in ethanol using an absolute method described in literature [25] using DPBF as a quenchers. SΔ, the efficiency of quenching of the triplet excited state by singlet oxygen is given by equation (2):
(2) SΔ = ΦΔ/ΦT

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of GQDs nanoconjugates
ce:section.ce:para: The conjugates (π)Pc-GQDs-(COOH)PS, (c)Pc-GQDs-(COOH)PS and Pc-(NH2)PS were synthesized in order to embed in membranes. They were not soluble enough for other studies to be done. The loading of Pc onto GQDs was determined spectroscopically using UV–Vis spectra as reported before [26], where (π)Pc-GQDs had a greater loading at 0.40 mg/mg compared to (c)Pc-GQDs at 0.14 mg/mg.

Fig. 1
shows the TEM images and corresponding size distribution histograms of the synthesized GQDs containing nanomaterials. The particles were monodispersed and the average size of the nanomaterials was observed to increase after conjugate formation from 14 nm for GQDs to 24 and 21 nm for (π)Pc-GQDs and (c)Pc-GQDs respectively. Gaussian distribution was also observed to improve following conjugate formation. These two observations may be attributed to further purification processes for the conjugates compared to GQDs.

Fig. 2 shows the FTIR spectra of the synthesized material together with their precursors (Pc and GQDs). Both Pc and GQDs possess the COOH group which was indicated by the carbonyl stretches (shoulders) at 1714 and 1692 cm−1 respectively, together with the hydroxyl bends in the region between 3300 and 3600 cm−1 for both materials. Upon conjugation carbonyl shoulders around 1698 and 1693 cm−1 were observed in the spectra for (π)Pc-GQDs and (c)Pc-GQDs respectively, which may be attributed to possible successful conjugation. The presence of the Pc peaks at 1598 and 1485 cm−1 (as tracer peaks) in the conjugates confirmed the presence of the Pc as an integration in the GQDs. These peaks are at 1596 cm−1 and 1487 cm−1 in the conjugates. The relative intensities of these tracer peaks also suggested a higher Pc loading in (π)Pc-GQDs than (c)Pc-GQDs which was consistent with observed loading values. Retention of bands in the region between 3300 and 3600 cm−1 suggests the presence of available OH, NH2 or COOH groups in the Pc-GQDs conjugates for possible further conjugation of (COOH)PS to (π)Pc-GQDs and (c)Pc-GQDs.

The Raman spectra of the synthesized nanoconjugates and their component materials are shown in Fig. 3 where the characteristic D and G bands of GQDs were observed at 1298 and 1454 cm−1 respectively. One main band at 1422 cm−1 assigned as the v 3 vibrational mode (asymmetric stretch) coupled to the vibronic band electronic transition [27] was observed in the Pc. For both conjugates there is a shift of both D and G bands to higher wavenumbers together with an increase in the D/G ratio accompanying conjugation (Fig. 3). The increase in D/G ratio suggests decreases in the surface sp2 domains in the conjugates. A greater D/G ratio in (π)Pc-GQDs than in (c)Pc-GQDs may be related to the position of the Pcs in the conjugates where in the former, Pcs were expected mostly on the GQDs surfaces, and on the edges for the latter. Observed shifts in bands also confirmed conjugation.

Fig. 4 shows the XRD diffractograms of the synthesized nanoconjugates and their component materials. The diffractograms show that all the components and conjugates were amorphous. However the diffractograms of the conjugates appeared significantly different, but each closely resembled one of the components. The diffractogram for (π)Pc-GQDs resembled that of the Pc while that of (c)Pc-GQDs resembled that of the GQDs. This observation may be attributed to the Pc loading and distribution on the GQDs. In (π)Pc-GQDs the Pcs were expected to cover the surfaces of the GQDs. This together with the higher loading (at 0.40 mg/mg) could have resulted in the conjugate exhibiting mostly the Pc properties. On the other hand the diffractogram for (c)Pc-GQDs may be attributed to the lower Pc loading and conjugation of the Pcs on the GQDs edges where they may have given the effect of peripheral extensions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of PS conjugates
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis of the photosensitizer-PS conjugates involved the amide linkage of the photosensitizer conjugates ((π)Pc-GQDs and (c)Pc-GQDs) to a functionalized PS ((COOH)PS), Scheme 1. To allow for elucidation of the effect of GQDs on Pc performance, the Pc was also directly conjugated to functionalized PS in the absence of the GQDs, Scheme 2. Direct conjugation required prior modification of (COOH)PS to avail NH2 groups for conjugation. This was achieved using hydrazine and was confirmed in FTIR (Fig. 2) by the shift of the carbonyl stretch of COOH in (COOH)PS at 1701 cm−1 to that of an amide at 1667 cm−1 while observing the emergence of an amide NH bend at 1643 cm−1 and an amine NH bend at 1601 cm−1.

Conjugation of Pc to (NH2)PS was confirmed by observation of shifts of the amide peak from 1667 cm−1 in (NH2)PS to 1657 cm−1 in Pc-(NH2)PS. The observation of a reduction in intensity of the amine NH bend at 1601 cm−1, while the intensity of the amide at 1643 cm−1 increased, together with spectral similarities in the range from around 1400 to 900 cm−1 between the spectrum of Pc and Pc-(NH2)PS suggest successful conjugation. Confirmation of the formation of (π)Pc-GQDs-(COOH)PS and (c)Pc-GQDs-(COOH)PS was mostly observed through the integration of conjugate photosensitizer spectrum with that of (COOH)PS. Since the amide carbonyl peak was masked by surrounding peaks, amide bond formation was not easily identified in FTIR. On the other hand emergence of the ester carbonyl peak at 1736 cm−1 in (π)Pc-GQDs-PS suggested possible conjugation through esterification. A corresponding peak was not observed in (c)Pc-GQDs-PS due to steric hindrance caused by conjugation of Pcs on the GQDs' edges which deemed the GQDs' OH groups unavailable for conjugation of (c)Pc-GQDs to (COOH)PS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of functionalized PS membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The SEM micrograms of the prepared membranes, Fig. 6 (using (π)Pc-GQDs-membrane as an example), showed the membranes to be asymmetric with a thickness of approximately 300 μm. The upper surface (top) consisted of pores with diameters less than 500 nm, while the lower surface (bottom) pore diameters were on average approximately 3 μm. The edge view showed that the membranes consisted of a less dense porous bulk and a thin densely porous skin, confirming the asymmetric nature of the membranes. The skin thickness was observed to be less than 10 μm in all synthesized membranes, and the pore density was observed through the upper surface pore sizes. Consistency in membrane structure and morphology confirms suitability of polymer solution compositions and membrane casting conditions.

The solid state UV–Vis spectra of all the prepared membranes (Fig. 7 ) show all the Pc characteristic bands to confirm functionalization of the membranes. The peaks are broad typical of solid state phthalocyanine spectra [30] due to aggregation.

The ΦF of the Pc in both (π)Pc-GQDs and (c)Pc-GQDs was enhanced compared to Pc (Table 1), which may be due to reduced non-radiative energy loss. The τF values were however observed to relatively remain constant. On the other hand, significant decrease in ΦF and τF of the GQDs in the conjugates suggested the possibility of Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) occurring. However for FRET to occur there must be a spectral overlap between the emission spectra of the donor (in this case, GQDs) and the absorbance spectrum of the acceptor (Pc). Fig. 5 shows very little overlap. Hence the decrease in ΦF and τF values of the GQDs, could be due to non-FRET processes such as changes in surface defects [31].

The ΦT value for (π)Pc-GQDs remained the same as for Pc alone, but decreased for (c)Pc-GQDs. The significant decrease in ΦT for (c)Pc-GQDs is possibly due to intramolecular rotations of the Pc in the conjugate [32–35] as a result of the relative rotational flexibility following conjugation of the Pc to the GQDs, when compared to (π)Pc-GQDs. There is also a decrease in τT of the conjugates in comparison to that of the Pc.

Despite the higher ΦT, a low ΦΔ value was observed for (π)Pc-GQDs probably due to the screening effect caused by the GQDs, which could have prevented the interaction of the excited triplet state of the conjugates and the ground state molecular oxygen [36]. The ΦΔ value for (c)Pc-GQDs corresponds to ΦT showing no screening effects. The difference in the ΦΔ generation behaviour between (π)Pc-GQDs and (c)Pc-GQDs could also be related to the differences in the size of the nano-conjugates (Table 1), there could be more screening effect for the larger (π)Pc-GQDs. A relatively higher energy transfer efficiency (SΔ) (Table 1) from the triplet excited state to molecular oxygen was observed in (c)Pc-GQDs following conjugation. During photo-irradiation for singlet oxygen determinations there was no change in the Q band intensity (Fig. 8 a), showing that the nanoconjugates were photo-stable while DBPF degraded.

Fig. 8b shows the UV–Vis spectra degradation profile of DPBF by (π)Pc-GQDs-membrane as an example in Φ Δ determination, where the absence of Pc spectral bands confirmed that photoactivity was due to membrane bound photosensitizers. The Φ Δ for (c)Pc-GQDs-membrane was observed to be approximately twice that of (π)Pc-GQDs-membrane. This observation suggested that covalent linkage to GQDs is superior to π-π stacking in application. On the other hand, observation of higher Φ Δ values in both (π)Pc-GQDs-membrane and (c)Pc-GQDs-membrane compared to Pc-membrane, confirmed that the use of GQDs as nano-supports is superior to direct linkage of Pc to PS.

The stability of the prepared membranes was studied by comparison of the membranes before and after subjection to light irradiations in singlet oxygen determination experiments. The absence of signs of deterioration in membrane structure in SEM images as exemplified by (π)Pc-GQDs-membrane (Fig. 9 a) suggests that the membranes were physically stable, while relative stability of the membrane solid state UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 9b) confirmed photophysical stability.

The use of graphene quantum dots as phthalocyanine nano-supports in stable photoactive polymer membrane preparation was successfully achieved through both π-π stacking and covalent linkage. The use of graphene quantum dots using both methods of conjugation enhanced membrane singlet oxygen quantum yields, but covalent linkage to the graphene quantum dots proved to be significantly superior and possibly most profitable for practical application.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology (DST) Innovation, National Research Foundation (NRF) South Africa and Sasol Inzalo Foundation through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620) as well as Rhodes University.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Heterogeneous photocatalytic oxidation of hazardous organic contaminants in water
Electrospun fibers functionalized with phthalocyanine-gold nanoparticle conjugates for photocatalytic applications
